title: 1. What is JavaScript?
content:
JavaScript is the programming language of the web. It allows you to create dynamically updating content, control multimedia, animate images, and much more. You write JavaScript code inside <script> tags.

The console.log() function is a developer's best friend. It prints messages to the web console (you can open it in your browser with F12 or Cmd+Option+I).
initialCode: Check the Console!

title: 2. Variables & Data Types
content:
Variables are containers for storing data values. In modern JS, we use let for variables that can be reassigned and const for variables that cannot.

Common data types include Strings (text), Numbers, and Booleans (true or false).
initialCode: JavaScript Variables

title: 3. Functions
content:
A function is a block of code designed to perform a particular task. A function is executed when 'something' invokes it (calls it).

Functions can take in parameters (inputs) and can return a value (output). This makes your code reusable and organized.
initialCode: JavaScript Functions

title: 4. Conditionals (If/Else)
content: Conditional statements are used to perform different actions based on different conditions. The if...else statement executes a block of code if a specified condition is true, and another block if the condition is false.
initialCode: JavaScript Conditionals

title: 5. Arrays
content:
An array is a special variable, which can hold more than one value at a time. Arrays are used to store lists of items. You can access items in an array by referring to the index number (starting from 0).

You can find the number of items in an array using the length property.
initialCode: JavaScript Arrays

title: 6. Loops
content:
Loops can execute a block of code a number of times. The for loop is the most common way to iterate over an array.

A for loop repeats until a specified condition evaluates to false. It has three parts: the initialization, the condition, and the afterthought (usually an increment).
initialCode: JavaScript Loops

title: 7. The DOM
content:
The DOM (Document Object Model) is a programming interface for web documents. It represents the page so that programs can change the document structure, style, and content. The DOM represents the document as a tree of objects.

JavaScript can be used to manipulate this tree, allowing us to make our web pages interactive.
initialCode:
The Document Object Model

JavaScript can interact with all the elements on this page.

title: 8. Selecting Elements
content:
To interact with an HTML element, you first need to select it. The most common methods are:

  • document.getElementById('id'): Selects a single element by its unique ID.
  • document.querySelector('selector'): Selects the first element that matches a CSS selector (e.g., '#id', '.class', 'tag').
  • document.querySelectorAll('selector'): Selects all elements that match a CSS selector and returns them in a list.
initialCode:
Hello!

This is some content.

This is more content.

title: 9. Manipulating Elements
content:
Once you've selected an element, you can change its content, style, and attributes.

  • element.textContent: Changes the text content of an element.
  • element.innerHTML: Changes the HTML content (including tags) within an element.
  • element.style.property: Changes a specific CSS style (e.g., element.style.color = 'red').
  • element.classList.add('new-class'): Adds a CSS class to an element.

title: 10. Handling Events
content:
JavaScript can react to user actions, like clicks, mouse movements, and key presses. This is called event handling.

The modern way to handle events is with the addEventListener() method. You tell it what event to listen for (e.g., 'click') and what function to run when the event happens.

title: 11. Creating & Appending Elements
content:
You can create new HTML elements from scratch using JavaScript and add them to the DOM.

  • document.createElement('tagName'): Creates a new element.
  • element.textContent: Sets the text inside the new element.
  • parentElement.appendChild(newElement): Adds the new element as a child of the parent element.
